findings.summary: performance.py houses two unrelated sub-domains in one module despite explicit acknowledgment that they should be split
evidence: Module docstring explicitly names two distinct sub-domains: 'Sub-domain 1 — Stage monitoring (psutil-based, pipeline-level)' and 'Sub-domain 2 — Function profiling (cProfile/tracemalloc, call-level)'

The rationale given is 'to avoid circular imports' — a structural workaround, not a design choice. The docstring even anticipates the split: 'If this grows further, split into infrastructure/core/stage_monitor.py and infrastructure/core/function_profiler.py'
PerformanceMonitor/StagePerformanceTracker and CodeProfiler/ProfilingMetrics have no shared state and zero interdependency within the file; their co-location is purely incidental
findings.suggestion: Resolve the circular import root cause (likely a logging or exceptions dependency) and split into stage_monitor.py and function_profiler.py as the module itself recommends. The acknowledged technical debt is the defect.

findings.summary: generate_pipeline_report unpacks ReportExtras field-by-field into local variables, then re-passes them, adding an indirection layer with no added policy
evidence: Lines 68-73: six consecutive assignments extract every field of extras into local variables (test_results, validation_results, performance_metrics, error_summary, output_statistics, project_name) only to pass them directly to PipelineReport and the log-file side-effect block
ReportExtras was introduced explicitly to group these six params — the function immediately flattens it back, making ReportExtras a no-op abstraction inside generate_pipeline_report
The log-file side-effect (lines 87-94) modifies output_statistics in place, mixing data mutation with report construction inside a factory function
findings.suggestion: Either pass extras fields directly to PipelineReport (removing the intermediate locals) or let ReportExtras carry its own to_pipeline_report() conversion so the factory stays pure. The in-place mutation of output_statistics should be a pre-processing step before calling the factory.

findings.summary: logging_utils.py re-exports constants and utilities from five split submodules, making it a wide aggregation facade with 30+ items in __all__
evidence: __all__ lists 30 symbols spanning formatters, progress utilities, ETA functions, spinner classes, constants — items that belong to distinct concerns

The module imports from logging_formatters, logging_helpers, logging_constants, logging_progress (twice) and _optional_deps — it is a pure re-export hub with no logic of its own beyond get_logger/setup_logger
Callers that only need get_logger must load the full import graph of all five submodules. The public surface is broader than any single caller needs.
findings.suggestion: Shrink __all__ to the core logging API (get_logger, setup_logger, log_operation, log_timing, format_duration). Callers that need progress utilities or formatters should import from their respective submodules directly.

findings.summary: Pipeline executor tests exercise the happy path almost exclusively; stage failure propagation and early-abort behavior have no cross-stage integration test
evidence: test_execute_stage_failure tests a single isolated stage throwing an exception, but there is no test that runs execute_full_pipeline or execute_core_pipeline with a mid-pipeline failure and verifies that subsequent stages are not invoked and results are truncated

_execute_pipeline breaks on the first failure (line 200: 'if not result.success: break') — this abort-on-failure path is the core correctness guarantee of the pipeline and has no integration-level test
test_skip_infra_execution checks invocation absence via JSONL log but does not assert on the returned result list length or stage ordering after a skip
findings.suggestion: Add a test using _create_fake_repo with one script that exits 1 and verify that execute_core_pipeline returns only results up to and including the failing stage, and that subsequent scripts produce no entries in invocations.jsonl.

findings.summary: CodeProfiler and the monitor_performance decorator in performance.py have no tests; only the psutil-based sub-domain is covered in test_performance.py
evidence: test_performance.py imports only PerformanceMetrics, PerformanceMonitor, ResourceUsage, StagePerformanceTracker, get_system_resources, performance_context — CodeProfiler, ProfilingMetrics, get_performance_monitor, monitor_performance, profile_memory_usage, and benchmark_function are entirely absent from the test file
CodeProfiler.monitor() contains tracemalloc coordination logic (we_started_tracing flag, conditional stop) that is non-trivial and completely untested
profile_memory_usage uses a result_holder list as a closure workaround and indexes metrics_history[-1] directly — fragile patterns with no test coverage
findings.suggestion: Add tests for CodeProfiler.monitor(), benchmark_function(), and the monitor_performance decorator covering at minimum: metrics are appended to history, memory fields are populated when track_memory=True, and the decorator preserves function return values.
